data.title: Majority of FOMC didn't see need to change 'easing bias' of Fed statement: Powell press conference
data.description:
"If we need to hike, we would certainly signal that, and we would do it," Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said during his post-decision press conference on Wednesday. Still, "nobody is calling for a hike right now," he added.

"Our commitment to (reducing inflation to 2% sustainably) is never-ending and unshakeable," he said.

The press conference comes after the Federal Open Market Committee again kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% for a third straight meeting.

Three dissents at the meeting favored a two-sided forward guidance instead of its mild “easing bias” in today's statement. 

"The center is moving to a more neutral place," Powell said. But the majority of the FOMC didn't feel it needed to signal that there's a potential for the next move to be a rate increase or decrease.

"Powell’s final press conference is effectively the official announcement of the end of the rate-cutting cycle," said Yimin Xu, on behalf of Seeking Alpha analyst Cestrian Capital Research. "The Fed is no longer concerned about the labor market and is firmly focusing on the impact of the energy shock alongside tariffs. Despite the huge uncertainties still ahead, the next move is most likely a hike, and the market is starting to price this in more aggressively."

Regarding the “easing bias” in the Fed statement, Powell said the FOMC is "working on the hypothesis" that tariffs would lead to a one-time price increase and fade over time. "We expect that to be happening in the next two quarters," he added. But the oil shock complicates the picture. "With energy, it's so hard to say," he said. Oil shocks tend to revert quickly. The policymakers want to see progress on inflation settling down before resuming rate cuts.

In explaining the reason for not removing the bias, he said, It's a form of forward guidance. "Markets are not confused about our reaction function," he added.

If the Iran conflict continues and that bleeds into inflation, then the Fed may have to increase rates rather than cut.

Developing… Check back for updates.

data.title: Seven Hills targets ~$950M portfolio by year-end 2026 while guiding $0.23-$0.25 Q2 distributable EPS
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Seven Hills Realty Trust (SEVN) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Yesterday, we reported solid first quarter results, reflecting the continued strength of our fully performing loan portfolio and our disciplined underwriting approach." (President & Chief Investment Officer Thomas Lorenzini)
  • "Distributable earnings for the quarter came in at $5.3 million or $0.24 per share, which was at the high end of our guidance." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "We reached a new high watermark with approximately $776 million in total outstanding loan commitments after originating 3 new loans totaling $67.5 million during the quarter, reflecting our continued progress in deploying the capital raised from our December rights offering." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "Originations so far in 2026 have been executed at a net interest margin of approximately 195 basis points, representing the highest level we have achieved over the past 4 years." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "As of March 31st, we had total loan commitments of approximately $776 million across 26 floating rate first mortgage loans." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "With recent loan repayments, we currently have approximately $110 million of cash on hand and nearly $400 million of available capacity under our secured financing facilities." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "Yesterday, we reported first quarter distributable earnings of $5.3 million or $0.24 per share, which includes $0.08 of dilution related to our rights offering in December." (CFO & Treasurer Matthew Brown)
  • "Earlier this month, our Board declared a regular quarterly dividend of $0.28 per share... we remain committed to this dividend level through 2026 at a minimum and expect distributable earnings to trend back to our quarterly dividend level by the end of this year." (CFO & Treasurer Brown)

Outlook

  • "Overall, we expect second quarter distributable earnings to be in the range of $0.23 to $0.25 per share." (CFO & Treasurer Brown)
  • "Although distributable earnings have not covered our dividend over the past quarter, we remain committed to this dividend level through 2026 at a minimum." (CFO & Treasurer Brown)
  • "We anticipate this quarter with the loans that we closed, the loans that we're closing... probably $200 million." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "We should have pretty good net portfolio growth, maybe $50 million, $75 million, something along those lines compared to where we are today. And then Q4 -- or Q3 and Q4, another couple of hundred million dollars of net portfolio growth." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "Ideally... we end up close to $950 million or so at the end of the year for total portfolio size." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)

Financial results

  • "Distributable earnings for the quarter came in at $5.3 million or $0.24 per share." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "Our weighted average all-in yield at quarter end was 7.8% and our weighted average loan-to-value at origination remained conservative at 66%." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "During the quarter, we received the full repayment of a $16 million loan... And subsequent to quarter end, we received an additional $54.6 million from the repayment of a multifamily loan in Ohio." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "We are also expecting the repayment of a $26.5 million loan secured by an office building in suburban Chicago as early as this week." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • "During the first quarter, interest rate floors remained active for 7 of our loans... These floors contributed $0.01 per share of earnings protection for the quarter based on SOFR as of March 31st." (CFO & Treasurer Brown)
  • "Our CECL reserve stands at a modest 130 basis points of total loan commitments, flat from last quarter... Importantly, we have no [ 5-rated ] loans, no collateral-dependent loans and no loans with specific reserves." (CFO & Treasurer Brown)

Q&A

  • Jason Weaver, JonesTrading: "...origination NIM of 195..." and asked where NIM settles; President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini said, "it's really product mix" and added, "the 3 loans that we're anticipating closing... net interest margin on those is... probably closer to about 180," citing multifamily as "where we're seeing the most compression." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • Jason Weaver, JonesTrading: asked about deploying liquidity and "realistic deployment time line"; Vice President Jared Lewis said, "the pipeline... averages about $1 billion" and added, "over the next 2 quarters, we should have the ability to kind of meet our targets of origination activity in that 100 to 300 over the next 2 quarters." (Vice President Lewis)
  • Christopher Muller, Citizens JMP: asked if SEVN wants more exposure to any asset type; President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini said, "we would certainly like to increase exposure further to multifamily," while adding, "the only thing we're not actively pursuing right now really is new office loans and health care-related assets." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • Christopher Muller, Citizens JMP: asked about geopolitical impacts and portfolio growth; President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini said, "With the war in Iran, things have slowed a little bit from a transaction standpoint," and outlined expected net growth and repayment assumptions. (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • Christopher Muller, Citizens JMP: asked about Yardley REO; President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini said, "Occupancy right now remains at about 81%, 82%" and "our goal there really would be... consider... dispose of the asset maybe late this year." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • Christopher Nolan, Ladenburg Thalmann: asked whether higher rates affect CECL; CFO & Treasurer Brown said, "we would expect our reserve at 1.3% of total commitments to be probably hanging around there for a while" and noted office repayments/maturities as potential mix-shifters. (CFO & Treasurer Brown)
  • Christopher Nolan, Ladenburg Thalmann: asked about construction input costs; President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini said, "our portfolio as far as future fundings is really somewhat limited... it's 6% of the total commitment" and added that budget overruns typically require "an equity rebalance" from sponsors. (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, pressing on sustainability of wider spreads, deployment pace amid large liquidity, and credit/reserving sensitivity to rates (e.g., "where do you see the rest of the year's NIM settling?" and "does CECL sort of require you to increase your allowance as loan rates go up?"). (Jason Weaver, JonesTrading; Christopher Nolan, Ladenburg Thalmann)
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, emphasizing selectivity and underwriting discipline (e.g., "we take a rifle shot approach" and "we remain selective in deploying capital"). (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • Versus last quarter: management messaging remained confident on dividend commitment and credit quality, while current-quarter Q&A introduced more explicit macro/geopolitical caution ("With the war in Iran, things have slowed a little bit"). (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Portfolio and deployment: Q1 highlighted a "new high watermark" of "approximately $776 million" commitments, versus Q4 ending commitments of "$724.5 million" and discussion of rights-offering capacity ramp. (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • Earnings and dilution: Q1 distributable EPS was "$0.24" with "$0.08" of dilution from the rights offering, versus Q4 distributable EPS of "$0.28" with "$0.03" dilution. (CFO & Treasurer Brown)
  • Guidance cadence: Q1 delivered results "at the high end" of prior guidance, then guided Q2 DE to "$0.23 to $0.25"; Q4 guided Q1 DE to "$0.22 to $0.24." (President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini; CFO & Treasurer Brown)
  • Market tone: Q4 described conditions that "continued to improve" with rate cuts and higher sales volumes, while Q1 emphasized "increased volatility" and "some moderation" tied to geopolitical and rate uncertainty. (Vice President Lewis)

Risks and concerns

  • "We have seen increased volatility across the capital markets, driven in part by the ongoing conflict in Iran" and "recent market volatility has started to have an impact on owners' decision-making." (Vice President Lewis)
  • "Competition remains elevated in certain sectors, particularly multifamily" and management pointed to disciplined pricing as mitigation ("we're not going to chase yields down"). (Vice President Lewis; President & Chief Investment Officer Lorenzini)
  • Dividend coverage: "Although distributable earnings have not covered our dividend over the past quarter," management tied mitigation to redeploying repayments and investing rights-offering proceeds. (CFO & Treasurer Brown)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a portfolio expansion and redeployment quarter, with distributable earnings at $0.24 per share and commitments rising to about $776 million, while emphasizing selective underwriting amid higher macro volatility. The company reiterated commitment to the $0.28 dividend through at least 2026 and guided Q2 distributable earnings to $0.23–$0.25 per share, with expectations that capital deployment and repayment-driven reinvestment support a move back toward the quarterly dividend level by year-end and a portfolio size near $950 million.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sagtec Global climbs 30%—FY earnings breakout or S&P 500 lag fill?
data.description:
Sagtec Global (SAGT) turned heads as the stock jumped ~30% to ~$2.21, and there are two major reasons behind this rally—strong FY25 numbers and a fresh AI push catching investor attention.

The company reported FY revenue of $19.1M, up 49% YoY, showing solid business momentum. EBITDA rose to $3.4M (18% margin), while net profit inched up to $1.8M.

In simple terms, the company's top-line growth stayed strong, and profitability held stable—enough to excite investors.

However, there was a small negative aspect too. Operating income fell to $2.1M (down 9% YoY) due to higher expansion and depreciation costs. But the market chose to look past this and focus more on future growth.

The other trigger? The company's new AI-powered SAGE AI platform, along with about ~$11M in early enterprise deals. The product combines POS, analytics, and AI-driven decision tools. This adds a strong AI angle to the business, which investors are clearly rewarding.

From a technical view, Sagtec Global's (SAGT) price rally looks event-driven. As seen in the TradingView chart, the stock surged above the recent resistance in the ~$2.6-~$3 area and then pulled back quickly, showing massive profit-booking.

The key level now is $2.1–$2.2. Holding above this can support the trend, while a drop below may weaken the move.

Moreover, SAGT is down ~17.87% YTD, and the S&P 500 (SP500) Index is up ~4.28%—so this rally looks like a catch-up move.

Other stocks to watch in the same space include STAK (STAK), SAP SE (SAP), Salesforce (CRM), Shopify (SHOP), Uber Technologies (UBER), and ServiceNow (NOW).

data.title: 'FOMC telling Warsh he won't have control' - TS Lombard’s Perkins
data.description:
The Federal Reserve’s decision Wednesday to hold interest rates steady should be viewed as an institutional warning shot at Kevin Warsh rather than a sign that current Chair Jerome Powell has lost control of the central bank, according to Dario Perkins of TS Lombard.

The rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.50% to 3.75% for a third consecutive meeting, citing uncertainty over the U.S. economic and global macro outlooks. 

The meeting produced an unusual split in voting, with four dissents to the decision—an unprecedented number for the committee.

Perkins rejected what he called “the dumb take” that Powell has lost control. Instead, he argued the “smart take” is that the committee is signaling to Warsh—President Donald Trump's Fed chair pick—that he will not have control when he takes the helm. Earlier, the U.S. Senate Banking Committee moved the nomination of Warsh for the role of Fed chair forward to the full Senate for a vote, where he is expected to be confirmed before Powell's term ends on May 15.

Perkins compared the Fed's move to “strategic voting” at the Bank of England, where policymakers sometimes use dissents to shape expectations before a leadership or policy transition. “This looks very strategic to me,” Perkins wrote, while adding that Powell would almost certainly deny that interpretation.

Powell said during the post-FOMC meeting conference he plans to remain on the central bank’s board of governors after his term as chair ends in mid-May, citing concerns about ongoing legal challenges to the institution, including the probe into the Fed’s building renovation project.

data.title: Lennox forecasts ~8% revenue growth while reaffirming $23.50-$25 adjusted EPS amid ~5% cost inflation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lennox International (LII) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Revenue was $1.1 billion, up 6% year-over-year" and "our segment margin was 14.4% in the quarter, down 130 basis points primarily due to the impact of factory under-absorption," said CEO Alok Maskara.
  • "Operating cash flow was positive $16 million and adjusted earnings per share for the quarter were $3.35," said CEO Maskara, adding that "in Building Climate Solutions, emergency replacement momentum and disciplined execution contributed to record quarterly performance."
  • "Growth from our Duro Dyne and Supco acquisitions completed in Q4 2025 contributed 6%," while "if inventory levels normalize, the segment profit was negatively impacted by approximately $15 million of manufacturing costs under absorption," said CFO Michael Quenzer.
  • "Innovation continues to be a critical differentiator for Lennox," said CEO Maskara, highlighting "a successful water heater launch" and "growing traction with new heat pump products" alongside product updates including "our new Strategos Rooftop with heat pump technology" and "high-efficiency Lennox heat pump water heaters via our Ariston joint venture."

Outlook

  • No management guidance vs. Street estimates comparison was included because the provided estimates data does not match the required fiscal quarter format.
  • "We are reaffirming our full year adjusted earnings per share guidance range of $23.50 to $25," said CEO Maskara.
  • "Revenue is now expected to grow approximately 8% compared to prior guidance of 6% to 7%," said CFO Quenzer, attributing the change to "modestly higher mix and price" including "the Lennox price actions announced earlier this week" and "the carryover benefit of the 2025 regulatory mix."
  • "Cost inflation is now expected to be up approximately 5% from up 2%, driven by recent increases in tariffs and input costs for aluminum, steel, copper and fuel," said CFO Quenzer, while "free cash flow remains expected to be $750 million to $850 million."

Financial Results

  • "Revenue was $1.1 billion, up 6% year-over-year," said CEO Maskara.
  • "Overall, HCS revenue declined 10%" with "organic revenue declined 12%," and "organic sales volumes declined 21%, but this represented a meaningful improvement from a 32% decline in the fourth quarter of 2025," said CFO Quenzer.
  • "BCS delivered another exceptionally strong quarter with organic sales up 26%" and "profit margins expanded 300 basis points," said CFO Quenzer.
  • "Free cash flow in Q1 2026 was a $39 million use of cash, an improvement versus a $61 million use of cash in the prior year quarter," said CFO Quenzer, adding, "inventory build in the quarter focused on parts and specific SKUs" and "we expect inventories to moderate from current levels in the second half of the year."

Q&A

  • Noah Kaye, Oppenheimer: asked about FIFO timing and first-half/second-half cadence; CFO Quenzer responded, "Most of the cost impact and the price impact would fall within the second half" and "overall, profitability should still be about the same as we reflected last year by the quarters."
  • Ryan Merkel, William Blair: pressed on April demand and why BCS guidance wasn’t raised more; CEO Maskara answered, "It's pretty hard to call quarter" and on BCS cited "the new factory is paying strong dividends" and "emergency replacement initiative... is paying off meaningfully."
  • Julian Mitchell, Barclays: asked about margin framework and HCS recovery; CFO Quenzer said, "we expect a slight decline in the margin" vs. January, while CEO Maskara said margin pressure was "100% driven by the factory under-absorption."
  • Jeffrey Hammond, KeyBanc: raised competitive scenarios on tariff-driven price; CEO Maskara replied, "We are very confident, and we have done price increases quite thoughtfully to make sure that we are not disadvantageous on share."
  • Deane Dray, RBC: asked about litigation; CEO Maskara read, "The lawsuit contains only plaintiff allegation and there has been no funding of wrongdoing... we will actively and vigorously defend our position."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone centered on tariffs, pricing power, and demand visibility, including "it's a little bit like the blind leading the blind" on Section 232 impacts, said Amit Mehrotra, UBS.
  • Slightly positive management tone emphasized stabilization and mitigation: CEO Maskara said "channel destocking has largely concluded" and CFO Quenzer said, "Overall, we feel good about the underlying momentum in the business while recognizing that the external environment remains dynamic."
  • Versus last quarter’s "cautiously optimistic" framing on 2026, this call leaned more to execution and cost offsets, with CFO Quenzer noting, "we have a proven track record of using multiple levers, including price and productivity to offset tariff-related cost pressure."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted on revenue growth: Q4 2025: "we expect total company growth of 6% to 7%," said CFO Quenzer; Q1 2026: "Revenue is now expected to grow approximately 8%," said CFO Quenzer.
  • Inflation assumptions moved materially: Q4 2025: "inflation is expected to be up approximately 2.5%," said CFO Quenzer; Q1 2026: "Cost inflation is now expected to be up approximately 5%," said CFO Quenzer.
  • Channel narrative progressed from destocking to normalization: Q4 2025: "2-step channel destocking is anticipated to be complete in the second quarter," said CEO Maskara; Q1 2026: "Channel destocking has largely concluded," said CEO Maskara.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The macro environment remains uncertain," said CEO Maskara, while "inflationary and tariff-related increases" and "fuel and transportation costs are also rising" were cited as cost risks alongside "the tariff environment continues to evolve with little notice," said CFO Quenzer.
  • On mitigation, CEO Maskara said Lennox is pursuing "productivity and reductions in material cost" plus "thoughtful pricing actions," and CFO Quenzer noted FIFO timing: "we do not expect any income statement impact from these new tariff rules until the third quarter."
  • On legal exposure, CEO Maskara stated, "we dispute the accuracy of the allegation" and "will actively and vigorously defend our position through proper legal channels."

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a return to year-over-year revenue growth with Building Climate Solutions delivering what CFO Michael Quenzer called "another exceptionally strong quarter" and Home Comfort Solutions showing sequential improvement in volumes, while guidance held adjusted EPS at $23.50 to $25 and lifted expected revenue growth to ~8% amid higher ~5% inflation and evolving tariff impacts that the company said it plans to address through pricing, productivity, and supply-chain actions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: ONEOK projects 2026 adjusted EBITDA midpoint of $8.25B as it raises net income outlook to $3.5B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ONEOK (OKE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Yesterday, we reported first quarter earnings and raised our 2026 financial guidance, reflecting strong performance and building momentum,” said CEO Pierce Norton (President, CEO & Director), adding that priorities remain “operate safely and reliably,” “execute our capital growth program with discipline,” and “maintain balance sheet strength and financial flexibility.”
  • Norton framed demand as structurally supportive, saying “LNG export capacity alone is projected to more than double over the next decade,” and emphasizing NGL linkage: “65% of U.S. natural gas production contains recoverable natural gas liquids.”
  • CFO Walter Hulse (CFO, Treasurer and Executive VP of Investor Relations & Corporate Development) said, “We are increasing our 2026 financial guidance,” and updated targets to a “net income… midpoint of approximately $3.5 billion,” “diluted earnings per share… midpoint of $5.53,” and “adjusted EBITDA guidance to a midpoint of $8.25 billion,” while stating, “Our total 2026 capital expenditure guidance remains unchanged at $2.7 billion to $3.2 billion.”
  • Hulse summarized Q1 profitability and items affecting comparability: “ONEOK reported net income of $776 million or $1.23 per diluted share,” and “Results included a noncash impairment of $60 million… related to our Powder Springs Logistics joint venture.”
  • COO Randy Lentz (Executive VP & COO) said “Winter Storm Fern created temporary wellhead freeze-offs that briefly reduced throughput,” but added, “there were no material downtime on our assets,” and highlighted projects including “the relocation of our 150 million cubic feet per day Shadowfax natural gas processing plant… to the Midland Basin,” with Permian expansions “increasing our capacity… by 110 million cubic feet per day,” and the Powder River “60 million cubic feet per day… Cutter plant… in the fourth quarter of 2026.”
  • Chief Commercial Officer Sheridan Swords (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer) pointed to volume momentum, including NGL volumes “increased a 11% year-over-year” in the Rockies, “increased 4% year-over-year” in the Mid-Continent, and “increased more than 30% year-over-year” in the Gulf Coast Permian region, and said “Refreshed request for capacity on our announced LPG export dock were already increasing and have accelerated more recently.”

Outlook

  • Hulse tied the raise to operating conditions and cadence: “These updates reflect strong underlying business segment performance,” and “As we move into the back half of the year, the combination of higher volumes, completed projects and market tailwinds should be reflected more clearly in our results for the balance of this year and into 2027.”
  • On quarterly shaping, Hulse reiterated seasonality: “We continue to expect the first quarter to be our lowest EBITDA quarter of the year,” and told analysts, “we expect the shape of that curve to continue.”
  • On 2027 setup, Hulse said, “we’re positioned very well to go into ’27 with a great tailwind,” citing operating leverage where “we don’t have any incremental CapEx that needs to be spent. All that’s going to drop to the bottom line.”
  • On power/AI demand, Swords said, “we remain in advanced discussion with several counterparties,” and Norton added that compared with earlier expectations, “the volumes that they’re requiring is going to require us to reach back further into our systems and lay larger pipelines.”

Financial Results

  • Hulse reported Q1 adjusted EBITDA “approximately $2 billion,” and said it was “a 13% year-over-year increase, driven by higher volumes and strong segment level performance.”
  • Hulse highlighted financing actions: “In April, we redeemed nearly $500 million of outstanding notes due July 2026,” and “we entered into a $1.2 billion term loan, further enhancing balance sheet flexibility.”
  • On commodity and hedging sensitivity, Swords said “realized commodity prices were lower in the first quarter as a result of entering the year fully hedged,” while Hulse stated, “Typically, we’re hedged about 75% going into a year.”

Q&A

  • Spiro Dounis, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked what drove the guide increase and what was locked in; CFO Hulse responded, “Winter Storm Fern was already in our guidance,” and the increase was “a blend of stronger volume expectations driven by higher commodity prices, continued expected differential opportunities.”
  • Dounis, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked about leverage timing and buybacks; Hulse said “most of our… larger CapEx will be completed by midyear of 2027,” and “that’s when we’ll really see the free cash flow kicking in.”
  • Theresa Chen, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked about producer behavior and timing; CCO Swords said producers are “leaning in to production,” “bringing on more completion crews,” and that added rigs are “a little more delayed into the back half of ’26.”
  • Chen, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked about export upside and LPG commercialization; Swords said on LPG dock contracting, “we are seeing an acceleration of interest,” and “we are not concerned at all about finishing the contracting.”
  • Michael Blum, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about blending hedges and Sunbelt Connector; Swords said “We came in highly hedged for the first quarter,” and on Sunbelt, “we think there’s only room for one project.”
  • Jeremy Tonet, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: asked about EBITDA cadence and 2027 recurrence; Hulse said “no change in the front end,” and for 2027, “our system is diverse, and we find differentials all the time.”
  • Robert Mosca, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: asked about FERC index exposure and guide sensitivity; Swords said “70% of our volume on the RPC system is market-based rates,” and Hulse said stronger curves were “not factored… into… guidance.”
  • Sunil Sibal, Seaport Research Partners: asked how much 2027 is hedged and data-center CapEx; Hulse said “we’re not going to get into specifics,” but these projects “originally thought they’d be $50 million projects, they’re turning out to be $400 million to $700 million projects.”
  • Gabe Moreen, Mizuho Securities USA LLC, Research Division: asked about ethane and Powder River/Northern Border; Swords said ethane economics are “very strong,” Powder River has “possibility to put some more capacity up there,” and “Northern Border is pretty steady.”
  • Gabe Daoud, Truist Securities, Inc.: asked public vs. private producer behavior; Swords said “we’re definitely seeing more on the private sector,” while Norton cautioned, “I don’t think we need to get too hung up on like numbers of rigs.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive and detail-focused, repeatedly pressing for “granularity,” “level set,” and guide “shaping,” including questions on hedging, differentials normalization, and project competition.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, emphasizing visibility and cadence with phrases such as “I just want to clarify,” “we expect,” and “we have not factored any of that into our thinking.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management used more constructive language tied to a guidance raise (“increasing our 2026 financial guidance”) rather than Q4’s emphasis on “disciplined caution around commodity prices.”

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved higher from Q4’s 2026 midpoint “approximately $3.45 billion” net income, “$5.45 per diluted share,” and “approximately $8.1 billion” adjusted EBITDA to Q1’s “approximately $3.5 billion,” “$5.53,” and “$8.25 billion,” while capital spending remained “$2.7 billion to $3.2 billion” in both calls.
  • Q4 highlighted acquisition integration and synergy capture (“nearly $500 million of total synergies”), while Q1 discussion shifted toward market-driven tailwinds, export demand acceleration, and larger-scale power/AI pipeline opportunities.
  • Analysts’ focus remained consistent on differentials, hedging, and capital allocation; Q1 added more emphasis on export infrastructure upside and data center/power project sizing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Weather-related volatility remained a key operational risk; Lentz said Winter Storm Fern “briefly reduced throughput,” though “no material downtime” occurred and impacts were described as already reflected in guidance.
  • Commodity and differential normalization risk was discussed, with Swords expecting “Waha to Katy differentials to normalize as new pipeline egress comes online in the second half of the year,” and management emphasizing hedging and a “predominantly fee-based model.”
  • Execution risk around large project delivery and contracting was addressed with repeated on-schedule comments, including Lentz stating multiple projects “remain on schedule.”

Final Takeaway

Management raised 2026 net income, EPS, and adjusted EBITDA guidance on “strong performance” and a “more constructive market environment,” while keeping $2.7B to $3.2B of capital spending unchanged. Leadership emphasized volume-led momentum across NGLs, refined products, and pipelines, ongoing export contracting strength (including accelerating LPG dock interest), and a growing set of power/AI opportunities that management said have expanded from smaller concepts into $400M to $700M projects, with free cash flow expected to build as major projects wind down by mid-2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CTO outlines raised 2026 core FFO of $2.06-$2.11 as structured investments target $175M-$250M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (CTO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO John Albright said the quarter was “highlighted by a robust leasing and strong same-store NOI growth as well as the $81.6 million acquisition of a high-quality shopping center in Texas,” and reiterated the focus on “shopping centers located along growth corridors primarily in the Southeast and Southwest markets.”
  • President, CEO & Director John Albright reported leasing, saying, “we executed leases, renewals and extensions totaling 153,000 square feet, including 146,000 square feet of comparable leases at an average cash rent increase of 14%,” and added that this brought the portfolio to “95.4% leased.”
  • President, CEO & Director Albright highlighted embedded growth, stating, “our Signed-Not-Open pipeline totaled $6.2 million of annual cash base rent,” which he said “will provide a meaningful earnings tailwind beginning as we move through 2026 and into 2027.”
  • President, CEO & Director Albright detailed capital allocation actions: “we announced an acquisition of Palms Crossing… in McAllen, Texas for $81.6 million,” and also said, “Madison Yards located in Atlanta is under contract with a nonrefundable deposit, and we expect the sale to close in May.”
  • President, CEO & Director Albright said structured investments expanded after quarter end: “we completed a $75 million preferred equity investment… This preferred investment yields 12% and has a term of 2 years,” and he summarized the overall impact as “a weighted average yield of 11.6%.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Philip Mays said, “For the first quarter, core FFO was $16.9 million… and on a diluted share basis was $0.52 per share,” and added, “AFFO was $18.2 million… and on a diluted share basis was $0.56 per share.”

Outlook

  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays raised full-year targets, saying, “we are increasing our core FFO outlook to a new range of $2.06 to $2.11 per diluted share and our AFFO outlook to a new range of $2.19 to $2.24 per diluted share.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays listed key inputs as “structured investments of $175 million to $250 million,” “same-property NOI growth for shopping centers of 3.5% to 4.5%,” and “general and administrative expenses of $19.7 million to $20.2 million.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s initial 2026 outlook, management raised the per-share ranges (previously “$1.98 to $2.03 for core FFO” and “$2.11 to $2.16 for AFFO,” as stated by Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays on the Q4 call).

Financial Results

  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays attributed growth in profitability measures, stating, “The growth in both core FFO and AFFO was primarily driven by leases executed over the past year that have since commenced paying rent,” while noting “approximately $0.01 related to nonrecurring recovery benefits.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays said property performance was led by retail: “same-property NOI for shopping centers increased 6.8%,” and “excluding the nonrecurring recovery benefits… still increased… 4.2%.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays described noncore drag, saying total same-property NOI growth was “impacted by one tenant… vacating 98,000 square feet at our Albuquerque property,” and reiterated mitigation timing: the space “has been fully leased to the state of New Mexico, which is expected to commence paying rent in late 2026.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays summarized leverage and funding actions: “total debt… $651.8 million,” “liquidity… $125 million,” and “leverage at 6.4x net debt to pro forma adjusted EBITDA,” and added, “we… issue[d] approximately 733,900 common shares… for total net proceeds of $14.2 million.”

Q&A

  • Jay Kornreich, Cantor Fitzgerald asked about the $75 million preferred investment rationale and funding; President, CEO & Director Albright replied, “We’ve already did the investment… it was one closing,” adding, “the Watters Creek was recycled into that,” and “we just use the balance sheet for the balance of it.”
  • Kornreich asked for an update on the remaining vacant anchor spaces; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “terms have been agreed upon moving to leases,” but “these things… go really slow,” adding, “conservatively… 3 months,” and “call it, 9 months or so to kind of get the tenant in place.”
  • Matthew Erdner, JonesTrading asked what would drive investment volume toward the high end; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “We do have structured investments… could happen here in the next 30 days,” and “we hope to be more active probably in the next kind of 4 months,” while Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays added, “if we complete some of the larger property acquisitions… it would push us up towards the higher end of the range.”
  • Erdner asked whether an outparcel pad (Forsyth) was in guidance; Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays said, “they won’t contribute to earnings in this year,” and it is “part of… $30 million of capital earning low double-digit yield unlevered.”
  • Craig Kucera, Lucid asked about a cap/target for structured investments as a share of the balance sheet; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “the cap… will definitely be below 20% and maybe more in line with the 15%.”
  • Kucera asked if yield ranges changed given a 12% preferred deal; President, CEO & Director Albright said acquisitions were “in the 7.5% to 8% range,” and structured finance “in the kind of 10% to 13% range.”
  • Kucera asked about leasing spreads given expirations; Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays said, “the spreads… continue in the range they’ve been,” and “for the full year, should be pretty good.”
  • Kucera asked about Signed-Not-Open timing/modeling; Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays said remaining pipeline is “a little more Q3, Q4 weighted,” and “you should get pretty much the full impact… in ’27,” with “one tenant… to early ’28.”
  • John Massocca, B. Riley asked if Madison Yards cap rate was ~6%; President, CEO & Director Albright responded, “It’s a little higher than that because of the AMC Theatres.”
  • Massocca asked if macro uncertainty is affecting leasing; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “There’s been no hesitancy… We have not seen any pullback whatsoever.”
  • Massocca asked about watch list changes; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “there’s been no notable change one way or the other on the watch list.”
  • Massocca asked how industry M&A affects CTO; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “helpful on our recycling side for sure, but not helpful on our acquisition side,” adding, “just being a little bit nimble is an advantage for us.”
  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners asked about Palms Crossing value-add and rents vs. market; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “the market rents are below market,” but “there’s not really any sort of play… anytime soon,” and pointed to “a little bit of vacancy” and “an outparcel that we didn’t pay any money for.”
  • Mehta asked whether Madison Yards is included in guidance assumptions; Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays answered, “No, we didn’t put a disposition volume out there.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral and detail-driven, focusing on capital allocation and modeling mechanics (e.g., exposure limits, yield ranges, pipeline timing).
  • Management’s prepared tone was confident, with President, CEO & Director Albright saying, “2026 is off to a great start,” and linking the raise to “increased guidance… to new ranges that imply approximately 12% growth at the midpoint.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, guidance language shifted from “initial earnings guidance” (Q4) to “increasing” outlook (Q1), while Q&A pressure remained centered on leasing timing, investment pacing, and capital recycling.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance increased quarter over quarter as Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays moved from Q4’s “$1.98 to $2.03” core FFO per share to Q1’s “$2.06 to $2.11,” and from “$2.11 to $2.16” AFFO per share to “$2.19 to $2.24.”
  • Structured investment expectations expanded from Q4’s “$100 million to $200 million” to Q1’s “$175 million to $250 million,” while the shopping-center same-property NOI growth assumption stayed at “3.5% to 4.5%.”
  • Capital recycling focus became more immediate with President, CEO & Director Albright stating Madison Yards “is under contract… and we expect the sale to close in May,” versus Q4’s broader discussion of potential asset sales to fund acquisitions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays flagged quarterly NOI sensitivity, saying, “$200,000 impacts quarterly growth by approximately 100 basis points,” and warned that “unusual and nonrecurring items… can occasionally skew our same-property NOI.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays highlighted a remaining earnings drag from the Albuquerque vacancy, while stating it is “fully leased to the state of New Mexico” with rent expected “in late 2026.”
  • President, CEO & Director Albright emphasized execution timing risk in large-box leasing, stating national tenant processes “go really slow,” even where “terms have been agreed upon.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a start-of-year acceleration driven by leasing, a new $81.6 million Texas shopping-center acquisition, and a post-quarter-end $75 million preferred equity investment yielding 12%. The company raised full-year 2026 core FFO and AFFO guidance ranges, anchored by assumptions that include $175 million to $250 million of structured investments and 3.5% to 4.5% same-property NOI growth for shopping centers, while pointing to the $6.2 million Signed-Not-Open pipeline and outparcel developments as multi-year earnings contributors.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Yum! expects Taco Bell U.S. margins of 24.5%-25.5% while keeping Pizza Hut strategic review on track for 2026 completion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Yum! Brands (YUM) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "In support of that mission, we recently unveiled our Raise the Bar priorities to drive our next chapter of global growth." (CEO & Director Christopher Turner) "These priorities include battling for the future consumer, accelerating restaurant unit economics and reaching the full potential of Byte." (CEO & Director Turner)
  • "In Q1, KFC delivered 6% system sales growth." (CEO & Director Turner) "the brand is driving system sales through industry-leading development with 7% unit growth in Q1 and a record number of Q1 gross builds." (CEO & Director Turner)
  • "Taco Bell U.S. reported 8% same-store sales growth, meaningfully outperforming the industry." (CEO & Director Turner) "Same-store sales growth in the quarter reflects 3 percentage points of transaction growth" (CEO & Director Turner) and "the successful launch of the Luxe Value Menu." (CEO & Director Turner)
  • "digital mix increased to 63% and digital sales approached $11 billion." (CEO & Director Turner) "This quarter, Taco Bell U.K. became the first international market to roll out both the digital ordering and Smart Ops bundles." (CEO & Director Turner)
  • "In the first quarter, Yum! system sales grew 6%." (Chief Financial Officer Ranjith Roy) "We opened 1,030 new stores in the quarter." (CFO Roy)

Outlook

  • Management did not state EPS or revenue guidance on the call, so no comparison to analysts’ EPS or revenue estimates was included.
  • "We now expect Taco Bell U.S. restaurant level margins to be between 24.5% and 25.5%, reflecting better top line momentum and higher margins than originally planned from the acquired Taco Bell stores." (CFO Roy)
  • "For this year, we expect our full year interest expense to fall in the range of $510 million to $520 million, excluding any potential debt issuances." (CFO Roy) "full year ex special G&A growth, excluding Pizza Hut, is expected to be up mid-single digits." (CFO Roy)
  • "we expect Q2 to reflect high single-digit growth year-over-year ex special ex Pizza Hut G&A due to the timing of project spend." (CFO Roy) "we expect approximately $5 million of noncash closure expenses for Habit" (CFO Roy) "by making a small number of closures in subscale markets." (CFO Roy)
  • "we expect Q2 core operating profit of approximately $17 million." (CFO Roy) "we remain on track to complete the review in 2026." (CFO Roy)

Financial Results

  • Management did not disclose quarterly EPS or reported revenue figures during the call, so no comparison to analysts’ Q1 EPS or revenue estimates was included.
  • "In the first quarter, Yum! system sales grew 6%. We grew new units by 5% and same-store sales globally at 3%." (CFO Roy) "Digital sales approached $11 billion with digital mix reaching a new high now at 63%." (CFO Roy)
  • "Taco Bell U.S. achieved restaurant-level margins of 23.9%." (CFO Roy) "KFC restaurant level margins were 10.3%, up to 100 basis points year-over-year" (CFO Roy) and "driven by a 240 basis point increase in U.K. restaurant margins." (CFO Roy)
  • "Ex special G&A was $284 million and was up 4%." (CFO Roy) "Franchise and license expense was $43 million, $9 million higher year-over-year" (CFO Roy) "primarily due to the investment in marketing and innovation testing as part of the Hut Forward program." (CFO Roy)
  • "For the quarter, gross capital expenditures totaled $75 million." (CFO Roy) "Our net leverage ratio ended the quarter at approximately 3.8x." (CFO Roy) "we repurchased approximately 1.2 million shares for a total of $185 million." (CFO Roy)

Q&A

  • David Palmer, Evercore ISI: "any color as you look around the globe in terms of demand trends"; CEO Turner: "We're pleased with the progression in KFC International" and "over the last 4 quarters, we've seen acceleration on a 2-year same-store sales basis" while adding that "those countries that I mentioned are proof points that his strategy is working."
  • David Tarantino, Baird: "are you seeing any signs of pause related to the unit growth"; CEO Turner: "As we look at our unit development outlook, we have high confidence." CFO Roy: "the entire Middle East represents less than 150 units in our current pipeline" and "as of today, our partners in that region expect no change to plans."
  • John Ivankoe, JPMorgan: "how you're thinking about this potentially big overall efficiency and effectiveness opportunity"; CEO Turner: "first and foremost, we want to use AI to drive growth" and described "10 different AI agents" in KFC U.K., including one "helping with permitting" and "Judy" helping "build training programs."
  • Dennis Geiger, UBS: asked about Taco Bell momentum and Live Más Café; CEO Turner: "They raised their aspirations" and "set a very specific target to strive for $3 million AUVs by 2030" while adding, "we're already getting tremendous returns from the Live Más Café investments."
  • Gregory Francfort, Guggenheim: asked about KFC comp disclosure and Saucy; CFO Roy: "we've moved KFC U.S. from being a subsegment" and noted it is "materially less than 5% of Yum! operating profit ex Pizza Hut"; CEO Turner: "we are already earning a big return on the Saucy investment across the global business."
  • Andrew Charles, TD Cowen: asked about KFC development risk and Taco Bell cadence; CEO Turner: "overall, no change to KFC's unit development outlook" and highlighted Taco Bell loyalty, saying "We had 30% growth in loyalty sales year-over-year" while CFO Roy added "we've taken up guidance on margins."
  • Hyun Jin Cho, Goldman Sachs: asked about Chief Scale Officer; CEO Turner: "We do still intend to add that Chief Scale Officer role" and said "We're taking our time to make sure we get that right."
  • Andrew Barish, Jefferies: asked about buying Taco Bell stores; CFO Roy: "we made, as you know, opportunistic acquisitions that are relatively small" and said the Q4 deal was "128 restaurants out of a worldwide base of 60,000 restaurants" while adding, "we're going to do it selectively and strategically."
  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Barclays: asked about QSR positioning; CEO Turner: "when you deliver what consumers want and need, you can win with consumers in any environment."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative to neutral analyst tone, with recurring pressure-testing on geopolitical disruption and development pacing ("geopolitical issues"; "LPG shortages") and structural questions on post-Pizza Hut cost structure ("bending the curve on G&A").
  • Slightly positive management tone, anchored in repeated confidence language ("we have high confidence"; "no change"; "we remain confident") and specific operating levers (AI rollout, Byte expansion, unit economics initiatives).
  • Versus last quarter, management maintained a similar confidence posture on Raise the Bar and Pizza Hut review timing, while Q1 included more explicit capital return and AI productivity examples ("half the size" development team; "10 different AI agents").

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q4, management framed 2026 as "confident the rest of our portfolio will meet or exceed every component of our long-term growth algorithm" (CFO Roy, Q4 2025); in Q1, CFO Roy repeated that posture with "we remain very confident in our ability to meet or exceed each component of our long-term growth algorithm when excluding Pizza Hut" while raising a key profitability outlook item: "We now expect Taco Bell U.S. restaurant level margins to be between 24.5% and 25.5%." (CFO Roy, Q1 2026)
  • Pizza Hut timing stayed consistent, shifting from "we intend to complete the review of options this year" (CEO & Director Turner, Q4 2025) to "we remain on track to complete the review in 2026." (CFO Roy, Q1 2026)
  • Q&A emphasis moved from Byte adoption mechanics and post-review portfolio framing in Q4 to Q1 emphasis on unit development resilience amid conflict, Taco Bell durability in a "difficult consumer environment," and organizational productivity from AI.

Risks and Concerns

  • "While the Middle East conflict creates uncertainty globally" (CFO Roy) and "has included short-term delays to obtain government permits and procure equipment in select markets such as the UAE and Turkey." (CFO Roy) "At this time, we see no change to our KFC development plans for the year." (CFO Roy)
  • Habit profitability pressure remained tied to input costs: "the team continues working to mitigate the ongoing bottom line impact of inflation, particularly from beef prices." (CEO & Director Turner)
  • Pizza Hut remains in transition: "Both Q1 and Q2 core operating profit includes expense related to the onetime Hut Forward investment that we previously disclosed." (CFO Roy)

Final Takeaway

Yum! management emphasized a Q1 start anchored in 6% system sales growth, 63% digital mix with digital sales approaching $11 billion, and 1,030 gross openings, while highlighting KFC’s 7% unit growth and Taco Bell U.S. 8% same-store sales growth. For 2026, the most concrete update was an increased Taco Bell U.S. restaurant-level margin outlook to 24.5%-25.5%, alongside reiterated expectations for mid-single-digit ex special G&A growth (excluding Pizza Hut), $510-$520 million in interest expense (excluding potential debt issuances), and Pizza Hut Q2 core operating profit of approximately $17 million as the strategic options review remains targeted for completion in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wingstop anticipates 2026 global unit growth of 15% to 16% while guiding for low single-digit same-store sales decline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Wingstop (WING) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We believe 2026 is going to be a transformational year for Wingstop and remain extremely confident in the long-term opportunity in front of us,” said President, CEO & Director Michael Skipworth, while noting “our same-store sales result in Q1 was disappointing and fell below our expectations.”
  • “Atypical winter weather resulting in temporary restaurant closures in over 700 restaurants” and “elevated gas prices as a result of the conflict in the Middle East” were cited by President, CEO & Director Michael Skipworth as the main Q1 pressures, adding that “our same-store sales trend worsened during the quarter and resulted in a decline of 8.7%.”
  • On execution priorities, President, CEO & Director Michael Skipworth said the company is pursuing “strengthening our operations through the Wingstop Smart Kitchen, expanding our reach to new guests and launching our new and highly differentiated loyalty program,” and reiterated a long-term “AUV target of $3 million.”
  • Chief Financial Officer Alex Kaleida said, “System-wide sales increased 5.9% to $1.4 billion in the quarter,” and added, “total revenue increased 7.4% to $183.7 million versus the prior year.”
  • On capital returns, Chief Financial Officer Kaleida said, “our Board of Directors authorized and declared a quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share,” and also noted, “the Board of Directors also authorized an additional $300 million available for share repurchases.”

Outlook

  • “We have updated our full year outlook… and now anticipate same-store sales to be down low single digits,” said President, CEO & Director Michael Skipworth, while adding, “we believe our business can return to growth in the second half of the year.”
  • Chief Financial Officer Alex Kaleida provided cadence assumptions: “We’re anticipating somewhere in the mid-single-digit decline range for comps in the second quarter, followed by a gradual improvement into that low to mid-single-digit positive range for the second half.”
  • Chief Financial Officer Kaleida updated expense guidance: “We are also updating our full year SG&A outlook to a range of $146 million to $149 million, which includes $3 million of restructuring charges… and $28 million of stock-based compensation expense.”
  • The company reiterated development and other items: Chief Financial Officer Kaleida said it is “reiterating… global unit growth of 15% to 16%,” plus “net interest expense of approximately $43 million” and “depreciation and amortization of approximately $30 million.”

Financial Results

  • Chief Financial Officer Alex Kaleida said “Adjusted EBITDA… was $65.4 million,” and added, “Q1 net income was $30 million or $1.08 per diluted share,” with year-over-year net income impacted because “a nonrecurring gain of $92.5 million [was] recognized in the prior year associated with the sale of our U.K. brand partner, Lemon Pepper Holdings.”
  • On franchise-driven revenue, Chief Financial Officer Kaleida said “Royalty revenue, franchise fees and other increased $8.7 million to $87.5 million,” and company-owned restaurant sales “increased by $2.9 million to $33 million.”
  • Cost and margin commentary centered on company-owned operations: Chief Financial Officer Kaleida said “company-owned restaurant cost of sales decreased 110 basis points… to 74.9%,” “primarily driven by a 160 basis point decline in food, beverage and packaging costs.”
  • Chief Financial Officer Kaleida tied higher SG&A to one-time items: “SG&A increased $3 million… to $34.4 million, primarily driven by a $2.4 million nonrecurring restructuring charge related to the corporate realignment announced in January this year.”

Q&A

  • David Tarantino, Baird: “where you’re seeing some of the traffic loss” and “any tactical response… to stop the bleeding”; President, CEO & Director Skipworth: “we do think that that’s attributed to… reaction to gas prices,” and on value messaging, “highlighting flavor under $10… not necessarily discounting or anything like that.”
  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Barclays: asked about comp improvement and confidence in “return to positive in the second half”; President, CEO & Director Skipworth: “we did see an improvement in the trend to start Q2”; Chief Financial Officer Kaleida: “mid-single-digit decline… in the second quarter… low to mid-single-digit positive… for the second half.”
  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Barclays: asked whether to “temper unit growth”; President, CEO & Director Skipworth: “we actually saw our brand partner margins and profitability improve in the first quarter,” and “we feel like we’re growing at the right pace.”
  • Christopher O’Cull, Stifel: asked about brand partners developing “ahead of their contractual mandates”; President, CEO & Director Skipworth: “we discourage brand partners from developing ahead of that contractual commitment.”
  • Sara Senatore, BofA: asked about low-income mix and “price point value below… $10”; Chief Financial Officer Kaleida: “about 25% within our database,” and “they’re actually trading up into larger bundles.”
  • Danilo Gargiulo, Bernstein: asked about adherence to 10-minute standard and capital allocation; President, CEO & Director Skipworth: “the change is pretty drastic”; Chief Financial Officer Kaleida: “we do see ourselves in a place that’s closer to that 4x leverage range.”
  • Jon Tower, Citi: asked if franchisees are reluctant to “move down on price points”; Chief Financial Officer Kaleida: “we don’t believe… training a guest to come to you for a $3 menu item… is… who Wingstop is.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, with repeated probing on comp deterioration, traffic losses, value posture, and whether unit growth should be moderated (e.g., “stop the bleeding,” and “tempering unit growth”).
  • Management: slightly positive in strategy framing but defensive on near-term demand, emphasizing external factors (e.g., “outside of our control”) while reiterating conviction in initiatives (e.g., “we have a high degree of confidence in this outlook”).
  • Versus last quarter: the tone shifted toward explaining a larger near-term comp decline (from Q4’s discussion of “stabilized” trends to Q1’s “disappointing” result), while keeping the same second-half recovery narrative tied to Smart Kitchen and Club Wingstop.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved down: Q4 2025 guidance was “flat to low single-digit percent growth” for domestic same-store sales; Q1 2026 guidance became “down low single digits,” explicitly reflecting “elevated fuel prices” and Q1 impacts.
  • Smart Kitchen discussion progressed from deployment/execution setup in Q4 to quantifying peak-period improvement in Q1 (e.g., “a 16-point improvement” in Friday/Saturday speed attainment and delivery satisfaction gains).
  • Q&A focus remained consistent on speed standards, delivery performance, loyalty impact, and value framing, but the current quarter added more direct scrutiny on traffic losses and macro sensitivity to fuel prices.

Risks and Concerns

  • President, CEO & Director Michael Skipworth cited demand pressure from “elevated gas prices” affecting “the lower-income consumer that our business overindexes to,” and weather disruptions that “resulted in temporary restaurant closures in over 700 restaurants.”
  • Execution risk was framed as change management: President, CEO & Director Skipworth said Smart Kitchen is “a meaningful operational transformation,” and “a better team member experience… but it is still a big change.”
  • Management’s mitigation emphasis was on operational and digital execution, including “targeting a launch of our order-ready tracker by the end of Q2” and preparing “for a national launch [of Club Wingstop] by the end of Q2.”

Final Takeaway

Wingstop management positioned Q1’s 8.7% same-store sales decline as largely driven by weather closures and a fuel-price shock to its lower-income skew, while keeping strategy centered on Smart Kitchen execution, a national Club Wingstop rollout by end of Q2, and continued mid-teens unit growth. The company updated full-year domestic same-store sales guidance to a low single-digit decline, maintained 15% to 16% global unit growth guidance, and emphasized capital returns through a $0.30 dividend and expanded repurchase authorization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: EXL forecasts 2026 revenue of $2.3B-$2.33B while raising adjusted EPS to $2.18-$2.23
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ExlService Holdings, Inc. (EXLS) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "In the first quarter, EXL generated revenue of $570 million, up 14% year-over-year and adjusted earnings of $0.58 per share, an increase of 20% year-over-year." (Co-Founder, Chairman & CEO Rohit Kapoor)
  • "Data and AI-led revenues grew 28% year-over-year in Q1 and now represents 60% of the company total." (CEO Kapoor)
  • "In Q1, total operations grew 10% year-over-year... The reported digital operations revenue after that migration was down 2% year-on-year. This is by design." (CEO Kapoor)
  • "We saw strong performance across each of our 4 operating segments to start the year." (CEO Kapoor) "Insurance grew 13% year-over-year" and "Healthcare and Life Sciences grew 21% year-over-year" and "Banking, capital markets and diversified industries grew 8% year-over-year" and "International growth markets grew 13% year-over-year." (CEO Kapoor)
  • "For full year 2026, we are increasing our revenue guidance to a range of $2.3 billion to $2.33 billion" and "increasing our adjusted diluted EPS to $2.18 to $2.23." (CEO Kapoor)
  • "We delivered a strong first quarter with revenue of $570.4 million" and "Adjusted EPS was $0.58." (Executive VP & CFO Maurizio Nicolelli)

Outlook

  • "We now anticipate 2026 revenue to be in the range of $2.3 billion to $2.33 billion... This also represents an increase of $20 million at the midpoint, which includes a $2 million foreign exchange headwind from our previous guidance." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "We anticipate our adjusted EPS to be in the range of $2.18 to $2.23... up from our previous guidance of $2.14 to $2.19." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "Our guidance is going to take... into the account what's happening in the current macro environment and also the geopolitical uncertainties that are out there." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "We don't really provide a breakup... between data and AI-led and digital operations." (CEO Kapoor) "But it would be fair to say that our digital operations will grow slightly below the company average, and our data and AI-led piece will be powering the growth of the overall company." (CEO Kapoor)

Financial results

  • "We delivered a strong first quarter with revenue of $570.4 million, up 13.8% year-over-year on a reported basis and 13.4% on a constant currency basis." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "SG&A expenses as a percentage of revenue increased 20 basis points year-over-year to 20.4%, driven by investments in data and AI-led solutions." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "Our adjusted operating margin for the quarter was 20.5%, up 40 basis points year-over-year, driven primarily by improved gross margins." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "Our cash, including short- and long-term investments as of March 31 was $266 million, and our revolver debt was $417 million for a net debt position of $151 million." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "We spent $13 million on capital expenditures and repurchased 4.4 million shares at an average price of $31 per share, totaling $136 million." (CFO Nicolelli)

Q&A

  • Bryan Bergin, TD Cowen: "Can you just dig in on the key assumptions for data and AI-led versus digital ops growth"; CEO Kapoor: "Some of the industries where we see a little bit of softness is on retail and on communication" and "it would be fair to say that our digital operations will grow slightly below the company average, and our data and AI-led piece will be powering the growth." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Bryan Bergin, TD Cowen: "Can you just comment any change in the expectation on adjusted op margin"; CFO Nicolelli: "You will see us... start to make additional investments" and "our AOPM forecast for the rest of the year will be similar... in that mid-19% range." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • David Koning, Baird: "Do you get some of them pushing you on price"; CEO Kapoor: "We are not really seeing clients come to us just asking for price reduction" and "the commercial model is also changing... towards a fixed fee... milestone-based payment and an outcome-based model." (CEO Kapoor)
  • David Koning, Baird: "Would you expect to see a little deceleration" internationally; CEO Kapoor: "We have very little and very limited exposure to the Middle East" and "we are not really seeing any direct impact due to the conflict as such." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Margaret Nolan, William Blair: "any perspective on net revenue retention"; CEO Kapoor: "on a net basis, we are seeing a much wider landscape to play in" and "the size of the operation actually increase despite providing them with a benefit." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Margaret Nolan, William Blair: "how [partnerships are] progressing"; CEO Kapoor: "the activity and the deal flow has increased substantially." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Surinder Thind, Jefferies: "help me understand the step down in the digital ops segment"; CFO Nicolelli: "As the adoption of AI increases, we are going to see there being a bigger shift towards data and AI-led operations" and "digital operations will likely continue to have the same kind of deceleration of growth." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • Surinder Thind, Jefferies: "turning to headcount"; CEO Kapoor: "our revenues have increased by 14%, and our headcount has increased by about 11%" and "you would expect the delta... to be 3% or so." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Abbey Hochreiner, JPMorgan: "specific drivers" for AI/data traction; CEO Kapoor: "we are seeing broad-based traction and growth across all of these different service lines" and "the challenge for us is actually being able to hire talent quick enough." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Abbey Hochreiner, JPMorgan: "10x harder" going from 50% to 80% automation; CEO Kapoor: "there's a whole series of work that needs to be undertaken" including "the data foundation" and "guardrails" and "token economics... very, very complex." (CEO Kapoor)
  • David Grossman, Stifel: "compare and contrast" NRR and durability; CEO Kapoor: "clients are actually getting more comfortable outsourcing more work and outsourcing more end-to-end process journeys." (CEO Kapoor)
  • David Grossman, Stifel: "can the NRR stay above 1"; CEO Kapoor: "I don't know. We'll have to see how that progresses." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Vincent Colicchio, Barrington: "what portion of new AI deals involve outcome-based pricing"; CEO Kapoor: "some of the AI deals have outcome-based pricing models" and "the biggest barrier... is clients don't have good metrics." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Vincent Colicchio, Barrington: "acquisition pipeline"; CEO Kapoor: "we are seeing a fairly strong pipeline of assets" and "we hope that we'd be able to kind of close an acquisition soon, but we can't really comment on the timing." (CEO Kapoor)

Sentiment analysis

  • "So good to see the raise" and repeated questions on "prudent outlook," pricing pressure, and digital ops deceleration kept analyst tone slightly positive but probing. (Bryan Bergin, TD Cowen; David Koning, Baird; Surinder Thind, Jefferies)
  • "We entered 2026 with strong momentum" alongside repeated caution language, including "we remain cautious about the current macroeconomic climate and geopolitical uncertainties." (CEO Kapoor; CFO Nicolelli)
  • Compared with Q4 2025, management tone stayed confident on AI demand ("we have excellent visibility" in Q1 2026 vs. "We have excellent visibility into 2026" in Q4 2025), while Q1 2026 Q&A leaned more toward mix shift mechanics ("digital operations... down 2%") and guidance prudence. (CEO Kapoor; CFO Nicolelli)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • "For full year 2026, we are increasing our revenue guidance to a range of $2.3 billion to $2.33 billion" versus the prior outlook of "$2.275 billion to $2.315 billion." (CEO Rohit Kapoor in Q1 2026; CEO Kapoor in Q4 2025)
  • "We are also increasing our adjusted diluted EPS to $2.18 to $2.23" versus the prior outlook of "$2.14 to $2.19." (CEO Kapoor in Q1 2026; CEO Kapoor in Q4 2025)
  • "Data and AI-led revenues... now represents 60% of the company total" versus "now represents 57% of total revenue." (CEO Kapoor in Q1 2026; CEO Kapoor in Q4 2025)
  • Analyst focus shifted from reconciling annual growth guidance and AI decision-making catalysts in Q4 2025 ("help us reconcile that") toward operational mix migration, pricing models, and headcount efficiency in Q1 2026 ("step down in the digital ops segment" and "outcome-based pricing"). (Bryan Bergin, TD Cowen; Surinder Thind, Jefferies; Vincent Colicchio, Barrington)

Risks and concerns

  • "We remain cautious about the current macroeconomic climate and geopolitical uncertainties." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "Some of the industries where we see a little bit of softness is on retail and on communication." (Co-Founder, Chairman & CEO Rohit Kapoor)
  • "The challenge for us is actually being able to hire talent quick enough to be able to fulfill that demand." (CEO Kapoor)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a start-of-year acceleration driven by scaled AI deployments, with the mix shift continuing by design as work migrates from digital operations into data and AI-led offerings. Executives emphasized that the commercial model is evolving toward fixed-fee, milestone-based, and outcome-based structures, while maintaining that client conversations are centered on measurable benefits rather than price cuts. With Q1 results, EXL raised full-year 2026 revenue and adjusted EPS guidance, while reiterating caution on macro and geopolitical uncertainty and signaling continued investment that keeps operating margin expectations around the mid-19% range.
